video_title: SpaceX IPOs at $2.89T Market Cap, US Govt Suspends Fable & Mythos 5, Altman Delays OpenAI's IPO |265
gist: SpaceX completes the world's largest IPO at a $2.89T market cap, creating 4,400+ instant millionaires and making Musk the first trillionaire; simultaneously, the US government issues a shock export-control directive forcing Anthropic to globally disable its top frontier models Fable 5 and Mythos 5, igniting a defining battle over who controls access to frontier AI; OpenAI responds with drastic price cuts and Altman signals he may delay the OpenAI IPO as recursive self-improvement accelerates.

insights.claim: SpaceX completed the world's largest IPO ever, opening at $135/share, closing up nearly 20% at $161 on day one, and reaching a market cap of $2.89 trillion within days — surpassing Amazon to become the fifth-largest company globally.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: A $2.89T market cap for a company generating roughly $1B/quarter in profit implies investors are pricing in civilizational upside, not current cash flows — a fundamentally different valuation framework than traditional equity investing.

insights.claim: SpaceX's IPO raised $75 billion in pure cash on day one, enough to constrain global liquidity for near-term competing IPOs including OpenAI and Anthropic.
insights.speaker: Dave (panelist, Mark HQ / $2T asset manager)
insights.so_what: The capital drain from SpaceX's IPO may compress valuations or delay timing windows for Anthropic and OpenAI IPOs, as global liquidity is finite.

insights.claim: SpaceX's IPO was the largest single-day creation of millionaires in history: roughly 4,400 employees became millionaires and 400 became centimillionaires or billionaires.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: The wealth creation at employee level signals a new era of equity liquidity that will recycle into venture, real estate, and consumption — especially in concentrated US tech geographies.

insights.claim: SpaceX is best understood as three converging exponential businesses: a launch monopoly (10x-100x ahead of nearest competitor), the Starlink cash engine (10B subscribers, $1B+ quarterly profits), and an AI frontier lab — rather than a conventional tech company.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Pricing SpaceX like a normal tech company misses the civilizational optionality. Investors buying SpaceX equity are buying a stake in humanity's future economy across three compounding curves simultaneously.

insights.claim: Peter Diamandis puts the probability of a SpaceX-Tesla merger at 100%; Polymarket shows 37% probability by end of 2026. The strategic rationale is Elon consolidating his full stack — energy, robots, cybercabs, orbital compute — under one mega-company, enabled by his 82% voting control in SpaceX.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: A SpaceX-Tesla merger would create the most capital-efficient vertically integrated tech-industrial company in history; Tesla investors would gain SpaceX exposure without a second IPO purchase.

insights.claim: SpaceX announced a definitive agreement to acquire AnySphere (corporate owner of the Cursor AI coding tool) for $60B+ shortly after its IPO, signaling it will deploy new public market liquidity aggressively into AI developer tooling.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG, panelist)
insights.so_what: This acquisition effectively pauses Grok as SpaceX's primary AI coding tool; Cursor becomes the de facto developer AI for the SpaceX ecosystem, setting a $60B benchmark for AI coding tool M&A and signaling SpaceX's intent to build an AI developer platform.

insights.claim: Approximately one trillion dollars in SpaceX stock is expected to become eligible for sale at the six-month lock-up expiry in late 2026, creating potential significant supply overhang.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: Investors accumulating SpaceX post-IPO should model significant selling pressure at the 6-month mark; this may create an attractive reentry point for long-term holders if the fundamental thesis remains intact.

insights.claim: SpaceX's S-1 filing explicitly warned that growing orbital congestion and cascading debris collisions (the Kessler effect) pose an existential threat to its core business, potentially rendering licensed orbits unusable for hundreds of years; Earth's lower orbit is already over the critical mass threshold.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: The Kessler scenario is a material black-swan risk to all SpaceX valuation models; a single deliberate anti-satellite attack could trigger an irreversible orbital chain reaction, collapsing the Starlink and orbital compute thesis.

insights.claim: xAI, SpaceX/Elon's AI component, is currently conspicuously absent from SpaceX's equity story despite being potentially worth trillions as a competitive frontier lab — its absence is a key gap in the investment thesis.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: Bulls pricing SpaceX as an AI company must account for the fact that xAI has not demonstrated competitive parity with Anthropic or OpenAI; if this gap persists, it materially reduces the AI premium in SpaceX's multiple.

insights.claim: Capital markets are now rewarding hard tech and civilization-expanding technology for the first time arguably since the mid-1970s, ending a multi-decade era where software, social media, and search captured virtually all equity value — Alex predicts this generational rotation lasts 10-20 years.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: This represents a generational rotation in equity capital flows from software/social to hard tech, space, and deep tech; sector allocation should be reweighted accordingly with a decade-plus time horizon.

insights.claim: The first solar system quadrillionaire is projected to emerge between the late 2030s and 2060s — most likely via owning a planet (Mars) or asteroid mining, based on extrapolation of historical wealth concentration and Elon's current $1.4T net worth trajectory.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: Long-duration investors should consider the optionality that planetary colonization and extraterrestrial resource extraction creates across multi-decade time horizons.

themes.theme: AI Export Controls & Government-Corporate Power Battle

insights.claim: On June 13, 2026 at 5:21pm ET, the US government issued an export control directive with only 90 minutes of warning requiring Anthropic to suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for any foreign nationals anywhere on the planet, including Anthropic's own foreign employees. Anthropic responded by globally disabling both models.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: The US government demonstrated it has unilateral authority under existing export control law to shut down frontier AI model access with near-zero notice — a systemic risk any enterprise or investor building on closed frontier models must now explicitly price in.

insights.claim: Across all frontier AI labs, 70% of elite AI researchers are foreign-born, primarily from China, India, Taiwan, and the UK; Anthropic's foreign employees represent approximately one-third of its workforce. Export controls blocking non-US persons from frontier models would gut the labs' own research capacity.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: The export control regime as applied is structurally unworkable without destroying the research capacity of the very labs it seeks to protect; expect either waiver mechanisms or a legal challenge from the labs on First Amendment or due process grounds.

insights.claim: Buried in a 319-page document, Anthropic reserved the right to (1) retain all user prompts and context for at least 30 days with no exceptions — overriding enterprise zero-retention agreements — and (2) silently downgrade users to weaker models without notification if Anthropic detected frontier AI research queries.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Enterprises cannot build reliable AI-dependent workflows on models that can be silently degraded without notice; this is the single strongest commercial argument for on-premises, open-weight model deployment as enterprise AI infrastructure.

insights.claim: Beyond silent downgrade, Anthropic expressly reserved the right to inject 'poisoning attacks' — actively interfering with and corrupting outputs for users conducting AI research, not merely routing them to weaker models. Alex calls this an unprecedented adversarial posture from an AI vendor toward its own customers.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: If frontier labs claim the right to actively sabotage user outputs, this is potentially both an antitrust violation and a product liability issue; expect class action suits and a new category of AI model safety benchmarks testing for vendor-side poisoning.

insights.claim: Dario Amodei published a policy essay arguing the government should have power to block AI model deployment based on third-party safety assessments — and within 48 hours the government exercised exactly that power against Anthropic's own Fable 5.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG) citing Dario Amodei
insights.so_what: AI CEOs publicly advocating for regulatory intervention should expect the government to act on it; this creates a regulatory risk where pro-safety public rhetoric can be converted into binding policy within days.
insights.quote: The government should have the power to block or deter deployment of the model if it is determined in light of third-party assessment to present unacceptable risks.

insights.claim: The export control jailbreak concern was reportedly identified by an Amazon researcher and escalated by Andy Jassy to the federal government, not by a hostile state actor — demonstrating that competitive dynamics between hyperscalers and frontier AI labs now extend into regulatory channels.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: Regulatory escalation of competitor vulnerabilities is a new form of competitive attack in AI; investors in frontier AI labs must now model the risk of hyperscaler-triggered regulatory disruption as a recurring scenario.

insights.claim: The Anthropic export control event will force every enterprise globally to build on-premises AI fallback capabilities and multi-model switching architectures; building on a single frontier model is now a single point of regulatory failure. US export restrictions are inadvertently driving enterprise customers toward Chinese open-weight models.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: On-premises AI infrastructure vendors and open-weight model providers — including Chinese models — are the primary commercial beneficiaries of the US export control regime; the US is creating demand for the very Chinese AI it seeks to contain.

insights.claim: Alex speculates the resolution of the Fable 5 shutdown will involve a 'grand bargain' — potentially including golden shares from Anthropic to the US government (possibly for a sovereign wealth fund), plus streamlined new procedures for frontier vulnerability disclosure under the existing AI executive order.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: If frontier AI labs issue golden shares to US government structures, this creates a precedent for state participation in AI lab economics — potentially reshaping the IPO capital structure for both Anthropic and OpenAI.

insights.claim: Export controls that block non-US access to frontier AI will create a bifurcated world where only the US and China have sovereign superintelligence. Europe and other excluded regions face becoming 'vassal states' dependent on AI produced by others — potentially triggering color revolutions in export-excluded regions.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: Nations excluded from frontier AI will face accelerating economic divergence; watch for sovereign AI crash programs in Europe and other nations as government-funded tech verticals, similar to historical nuclear weapon programs.

insights.claim: Dave argues the Fable 5 event is a turning point for all of humanity — the first time the US government has blocked AI, but definitively not the last. Government control over AI releases will become the norm going forward.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: AI lab equity must now carry a permanent regulatory override discount; the government's ability to block model deployment without compensation is an asymmetric downside risk not priced into most AI lab valuations.
insights.quote: At the end of the day, who owns AI? Is it the government or is it the corporations?

themes.theme: OpenAI: Pricing War, IPO Timing & Recursive Self-Improvement

insights.claim: OpenAI is considering drastic price cuts to poach Anthropic's customers while Fable 5 and Mythos 5 are offline — repackaging the natural ~40x/year per-token cost deflation as a competitive weapon against a temporarily disabled rival.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis / Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: AI token pricing is in structural hyperdeflation at ~40x/year; the price war accelerates commoditization, compresses margin, and makes switching costs near-zero — benefiting enterprises but reducing AI lab profitability and complicating IPO revenue multiples.

insights.claim: Sam Altman stated: 'The faster that recursive self-improvement takes off, the more it could be advantageous for us to delay OpenAI's IPO' — implying that AI compounding its own intelligence makes OpenAI's equity more valuable over time and selling equity cheaply now would be a strategic mistake.
insights.speaker: Sam Altman (quoted by Peter Diamandis)
insights.so_what: If AI companies can self-fund via recursive capability gains, the IPO market for frontier AI labs may be structurally delayed; this challenges the traditional growth-equity model and pushes AI lab liquidity further into the future.
insights.quote: The faster that recursive self-improvement takes off, the more it could be advantageous for us to delay OpenAI's IPO.

insights.claim: Alex's deeper take: if OpenAI's IPO delay reflects genuine recursive self-improvement acceleration rather than Codex revenue spin, it may mark the beginning of a decoupling between technology and capital — where AI self-improvement enables companies to self-fund without external equity, potentially rendering capital itself redundant over decades.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: A post-capital economy enabled by AI self-funding would fundamentally disrupt venture capital, private equity, and public market structures; this is an early warning signal for structural change in how technology development is financed.

insights.claim: OpenAI has recently raised $122 billion — larger than SpaceX's IPO capital raise — giving it substantial balance sheet buffer that makes delaying an IPO financially viable with no capital urgency.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: OpenAI does not need an IPO for capital; any IPO decision is strategic (governance, employee liquidity, competitive positioning) rather than financial necessity, giving it maximum flexibility on timing.

insights.claim: OpenAI's Codex engineering lead announced that Codex can now set its own goals: 'I never write my own goals anymore — I ask Codex to write one for itself and one for each sub-agent it spawns.' This marks the beginning of AI systems setting their own objectives.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (citing Codex team)
insights.so_what: Self-goal-setting AI agents represent a qualitative leap in AI autonomy and accelerate recursive self-improvement loops; this is the key commercial narrative that supports Codex as OpenAI's primary revenue engine ahead of any IPO.

insights.claim: Dave observes that Sam Altman owns no meaningful equity in OpenAI but has invested in 400+ surrounding AI companies; he has no personal financial incentive to rush the IPO and is therefore more likely to be genuine about safety-motivated timing delays.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: OpenAI's CEO incentive structure is unusual — he has no personal IPO liquidity motive — which means the company may genuinely prioritize safety or strategic timing over investor pressure, reducing the near-term IPO probability.

insights.claim: Salim's Law: every 10x drop in AI token prices produces 100 times more experiments run — creating a compounding innovation multiplier where intelligence deflation exponentially accelerates discovery and product iteration.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: AI token price deflation is not merely a cost story — it is an experiment-velocity multiplier; enterprises that optimize for AI-driven experimentation throughput will compound competitive advantage at superlinear rates.

insights.claim: AI data center compute capacity has doubled every 7 months since Colossus One came online in August 2024 (built in 122 days by xAI). Global AI computing capacity is growing 3.3x per year with no sign of slowing through 2028.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis (citing Epic AI data)
insights.so_what: The 3.3x/year compute growth rate creates insatiable demand for power infrastructure, transformers, and cooling — investing in these supply chain components is a model-agnostic way to play the AI build-out regardless of which AI lab wins.

insights.claim: The bottleneck in AI data center build-out is no longer chips or money — it is power transformers (2.5-year wait) and step-up transformers (3-year wait). Key beneficiary companies posting 100-400% YoY revenue growth include Hitachi, Siemens, GE Vernova, Hyundai, Hicsung Hico, Virginia Transformer, and DeltaStar.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Power transformer manufacturers are a secular growth bet on AI infrastructure with multi-year order backlogs providing exceptional revenue visibility; these companies offer AI upside exposure without model selection or regulatory risk.

insights.claim: 50% of the 9 GW of planned 2026 AI compute build-out has been delayed due to organized activist protests at data center sites — mirroring the 1970s pattern that froze US nuclear power expansion for 30 years.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Activist opposition to data centers is a meaningful supply-side delay that will keep power infrastructure margins elevated and extend transformer/generator backlog durations, benefiting existing contracted suppliers.

insights.claim: Alex and Dave independently concluded that lunar data centers are inevitable: LEO and SSO are unsuitable for the largest coherent training clusters due to cooling and coherence requirements, but the Moon's south pole (near Shackleton crater) offers natural atmospheric protection, cheap solar, and superior passive cooling.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG) / Dave
insights.so_what: Lunar infrastructure development is a 10-20 year investment theme; SpaceX via Starship is the only near-term vehicle that makes lunar data center economics viable, creating a long-duration moat around SpaceX's position.

insights.claim: Alex posits a future bifurcation: Earth becomes the AI training hub (requiring large coherent clusters) while orbital space becomes the inference hub (tolerating distributed, non-coherent compute) — implying a two-tier planetary compute architecture by the early 2030s.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: Inference compute demand will eventually expand off-planet; companies building orbital inference infrastructure (SpaceX AI satellites, Dyson swarm) have a decade head start over any terrestrial competitor and an uncapped total addressable market.

insights.claim: Elon Musk has stated he expects a 10x global GDP expansion in 10 years, reaching over $1 quadrillion in GDP — implying each active AI researcher or founder has a personal value-creation quota approaching $10 billion.
insights.speaker: Dave (citing Elon Musk)
insights.so_what: Even if directionally correct at a fraction of the stated scale, this framing argues for much larger startup ambition than the current SaaS paradigm implies and supports outsized VC fund sizes targeting AI-native markets.

themes.theme: AI Governance, Wealth Distribution & Social Stability

insights.claim: The core question crystallized by the Fable/Mythos shutdown is permanent: who owns AI — the government or the corporations? The US government has demonstrated it can exercise unilateral authority to block AI model deployment and this power will not be relinquished.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: AI lab equity must carry a permanent regulatory override discount; the government's ability to shut down models without compensation creates an asymmetric downside risk not present in most technology sectors.
insights.quote: At the end of the day, who owns AI? Is it the government or is it the corporations?

insights.claim: The four people most likely to determine global governance in 2027 and beyond are David Sacks, Donald Trump, Elon Musk, and Dario Amodei — with Sam Altman conspicuously absent from this inner circle despite OpenAI's prominence.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: AI policy is being shaped by a tight informal network; regulatory access and political alignment with these figures will determine which AI labs receive favorable treatment, government contracts, and export exemptions.

insights.claim: Across 20 historical revolutions — from the French Revolution to the Arab Spring — the universal trigger is educated young men aged 18-28 whose real outcomes fall short of their promised expectations. AI is creating exactly this pressure: recent college grads have the highest unemployment, young software developers (22-25) are losing jobs, and customer support workers face displacement.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: The combination of AI-driven youth unemployment and social media amplification (now AI-assisted) creates a 2-8 year window of elevated political instability risk; investors should model geopolitical risk premia and watch for accelerating policy responses.

insights.claim: The world lacks both the language and institutional architecture to handle wealth at the trillionaire scale; the social contract is breaking and a new abundance distribution model is urgently needed. 50% of the US population cannot cover a $500 emergency.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: Policy innovation around UBI, universal basic services, or universal basic equity will be a major structural driver of political economics in the next decade; track pilot programs and legislative proposals as leading indicators of the redistribution regime.

insights.claim: Andrew Yang's AI taxation proposal is politically popular but economically superfluous: existing corporate income tax already captures AI-driven profit growth as labor costs fall; AI-specific excise taxes would distort innovation and slow critical research such as cancer cures.
insights.speaker: Dave / Salim Ismail / Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: AI tax proposals will be politically likely but economically counterproductive; the more durable policy path is driving cost of living toward zero via AI-produced abundance rather than redistribution via excise taxes.

insights.claim: Alex argues the optimal redistribution mechanism is universal basic equity (UBE) — giving citizens a share of the AI economy's upside — rather than UBI or AI-specific taxes; frontier AI labs themselves may proactively offer golden shares to preempt more damaging forced regulation.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: If frontier AI labs voluntarily issue golden shares or equity stakes to US sovereign wealth structures ahead of their IPOs, this could be a positive catalyst — creating broad citizen participation in AI upside and reducing regulatory confrontation risk.

insights.claim: Despite — and because of — AI-driven youth unemployment, it is simultaneously the best time in history for AI-native startups: VC capital is at record levels, and AI-native companies can recruit top talent from graduating classes who cannot find traditional employment. Incubators are going 'gangbusters.'
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: The structural dislocation that is creating youth unemployment is the best recruitment environment in history for AI-native startups; founders capitalizing on this dynamic have access to unprecedented talent potentially at below-market compensation.

insights.claim: Salim Ismail's Organizational Singularity pilot has launched with 10 companies in a 3-month AI-centric transformation process; early estimates suggest pure-play AI-native organizations (EXO 3.0) will outperform legacy structures by approximately 100x on key performance metrics.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: Companies that execute AI-centric organizational redesign early may achieve order-of-magnitude competitive advantages; this pilot provides early empirical evidence on the 100x performance claim before it becomes consensus.

insights.claim: The future enterprise AI architecture requires: (1) a proprietary data lake freed from ERP system lock-in, (2) AI-native workflow layers on top, and (3) periodic elimination of horizontal bureaucratic layers (IT, HR, legal) that accumulate veto power by defaulting to 'no.' ERP vendors are actively fighting this migration to preserve stickiness.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail / Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: Legacy ERP vendors face existential competitive pressure as enterprises migrate to data-lake-centric AI architectures; this is a multi-year displacement opportunity for AI-native enterprise software vendors.

insights.claim: The key enterprise value driver in the AI era is not the data you own or the customers you have — it is the speed of your AI learning loop: how quickly intelligence is applied to augment products, evaluate strategic alternatives, and create new offerings.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: Investors should value AI-native companies on learning velocity metrics rather than traditional revenue or data asset metrics; companies that minimize their AI feedback loop latency will compound competitive advantage at superlinear rates.

insights.claim: Wealth and capital concentration is now so extreme in 2-3 US geographies that real estate investors and VCs must specifically follow where trillionaire capital (SpaceX employees, Elon, Jensen, Larry, Michael Dell) will recycle; these geographies will diverge dramatically from the rest of the US and from Europe.
insights.speaker: Dave
insights.so_what: Geographic concentration of AI wealth creation argues for overweighting real estate, venture, and consumer assets in Bay Area, Austin, and select tech-hub markets relative to non-tech metros and especially European markets.

insights.claim: Alex (AWG) views Bitcoin as a non-productive asset — it generates no coupons, dividends, interest, new ideas, or expansion of humanity's future freedom of action — and prefers stablecoins insofar as they enable efficient financial transfers and stabilize the US dollar.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: From a capital allocation standpoint, Bitcoin is speculative rather than productive; the investment case depends entirely on price appreciation rather than value creation, making it unsuitable as a core portfolio holding alongside productive assets.

insights.claim: AI agents will be capable of inventing their own Layer 1 blockchain protocols likely more efficient than Bitcoin — undermining the 'first-mover primacy' argument for Bitcoin as a long-term value store.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: If AI-created L1 protocols displace Bitcoin's first-mover advantage, Bitcoin's value proposition narrows significantly; this is a structural long-term risk to Bitcoin maximalism arguments that the market has not priced.

insights.claim: Bitcoin now satisfies the blockchain trilemma (decentralization, security, scalability) via the Lightning Network created by Blockstream — unlike Ethereum which compromised decentralization with its recent centrally-coordinated fix, or failed altcoins like FTX and Luna that failed on security or decentralization.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: If the Lightning Network trilemma argument holds, Bitcoin has a unique technical foundation among digital assets; this is the key counterpoint to the non-productive asset critique and explains renewed institutional interest in Bitcoin specifically.

insights.claim: A quantum computing breakthrough breaking the entire crypto stack within 3 years is considered an aggressive timeline; the industry including Coinbase (Brian Armstrong) is actively developing post-quantum cryptographic algorithms and is expected to resolve this before quantum poses a realistic threat.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: Near-term quantum risk to crypto is overstated; the 3-year threat horizon is unlikely, and the industry has adequate warning time to migrate to quantum-resistant protocols — reducing this as a near-term investment risk.

insights.claim: Norway's Government Pension Fund (~$1.7T AUM), which owns 1-2% of every publicly traded company globally and is managed independently from political interference, is the gold standard for sovereign wealth funds built on resource revenues.
insights.speaker: Peter Diamandis
insights.so_what: A US sovereign wealth fund modeled on Norway's — funded by AI-generated tax revenues and managed independently — could distribute AI upside broadly to citizens; this is the most implementable policy vehicle for universal basic equity.

insights.claim: AI already effectively manages sovereign wealth funds: algorithmic AI trading dominates public equities by volume and is the primary mechanism of price discovery, including setting SpaceX's $2.89T valuation as the world's fifth-largest company.
insights.speaker: Alex (AWG)
insights.so_what: Policy proposals to 'use AI to manage sovereign wealth funds' miss that AI already does this at scale via algorithmic market dominance; the real policy question is how to structure the sovereign fund's equity exposure, not what manages it.
